1: she’alu meYHWH matar be’et matar malqosh YHWH oseh chazizim umtar geshem yiten lahem le’ish esev basadeh: ‘Ask rain from the LORD in the season of the spring rain. The LORD is the one who makes the storm clouds; he will give them showers of rain and grass in the fields for everyone.’ Malkosh (spring rain / latter rain) is the crucial rain that falls in March-April, completing the grain’s growth before harvest. The call to ask YHWH specifically for rain (rather than consulting the teraphim of Zechariah 10:2) frames Zechariah 10:1-2 as a contrast: YHWH gives the actual rain; idols give only worthless speech. The theological principle: the one who controls the rain controls the food supply, and therefore all political and economic power ultimately derives from YHWH — the point Elijah made against Baal on Carmel (1 Kgs 17-18).
2: ki hateraphim dibru aven vehaqqosemim chazu shaqer vachalomot hashav yedaberu hevel yenachamun: ‘For the household idols speak worthlessness, and the diviners see false visions; they tell lying dreams and give empty comfort.’ The teraphim (household gods, small figurines used for divination and protection) are contrasted with YHWH who gives real rain and real help. Aven (worthlessness/trouble), shaqer (falsehood), shav (emptiness/vanity), hevel (breath/vapor — the Ecclesiastes word for meaninglessness) — the four terms are a lexicon of futility. The idols’ comfort (yenachamun) is empty because they cannot control the physical world they claim to govern; only YHWH who makes the rain can offer real consolation.
3: al haro’im chara appi ve’al ha’attudim eqod ki paqad YHWH tzvaot et edro et beit Yehudah: ‘My anger burns against the shepherds, and I will punish the leaders; for the LORD of Hosts tends his flock, the house of Judah, and will make them like his majestic horse in battle.’ Ro’im (shepherds) and attudim (he-goats, leaders/chief men) are parallel terms for the ruling class — using the pastoral/flock metaphor for political leadership (common in the ancient Near East). YHWH’s punishment of the negligent shepherds leads directly to his own shepherding of the flock (Ezek 34 develops this extensively). The transformation of the flock from sheep to a war-horse (sus hod, majestic/stately battle horse) is the reversal of helplessness into strength through YHWH’s care.
4: mimenu pinnah mimenu yated mimenu qeshet milchamah mimenu yetze kol noges yachdav: ‘From Judah will come the cornerstone, from him the tent peg, from him the battle bow, from him every ruler — all of them together.’ Three structural images — pinnah (cornerstone, the structural foundation stone), yated (tent peg, the anchor pin that holds the structure), and qeshet milchamah (battle bow, the offensive military weapon) — describe the comprehensive messianic leadership arising from Judah. The cornerstone is the key Christological term: Isaiah 28:16 (I lay in Zion a cornerstone, a tested stone, a precious cornerstone); Psalms 118:22 (the stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone); and Jesus’s own citation of Psalms 118:22 (Matthew 21:42) plus Peter (1 Peter 2:6-7) and Paul (Ephesians 2:20) apply the cornerstone directly to Christ.
5: vehayu kegibborim boshim barei chutzot bamilchamah: ‘Together they will be like mighty warriors in battle, trampling their enemies in the street mud. They will fight because the LORD is with them.’ The revival of the Judean warriors — transformed from scattered sheep to mighty warriors — is YHWH’s military empowerment of his people. The critical phrase is ki YHWH imam (for the LORD is with them) — the Immanuel principle as the ground for military effectiveness. The battle capability does not originate in the people’s own strength but in the divine presence: “not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit” (Zechariah 4:6) is the theological framework governing all the battle imagery.
6: vegibbarti et beit Yehudah ve’et beit Yosef oshi’a veoshivotim ki richamtim: ‘I will strengthen the house of Judah and save the house of Joseph. I will bring them back, for I have compassion on them.’ Richamtim (I have compassion on them) — from rachamim (compassion, womb-love, the deepest maternal/covenant love). The reunification of Judah and Joseph (= Ephraim = the northern kingdom) is the eschatological reversal of the kingdom division (931 BCE) and the Assyrian deportation (722 BCE): the full twelve-tribe Israel restored through divine compassion. The promise ki anochi YHWH Eloheihem ve’e’eneh otam (for I am YHWH their God and I will answer them) is the covenant self-identification as the ground of the promise.
7: vehayu chigibor Efraim vesamas libam kayayin uveneihem yiru visamechu yagel libam baYHWH: ‘Ephraim will be like a mighty warrior, and their hearts will rejoice as with wine. Their children will see it and be glad; their hearts will rejoice in the LORD.’ The joy of restoration is expressed through the wine-gladness metaphor (yism’as libam kayayin, their hearts will rejoice as with wine) — the exhilarating joy of warriors victorious in YHWH’s strength. The intergenerational dimension — children seeing and rejoicing — emphasizes that the restoration is comprehensive and lasting, affecting future generations, not just the present survivors. The joy is specifically in YHWH (yagel libam baYHWH), not in the victory itself.
8: eshrqah lahem ve’eqobbetzem ki pedaytim verabu kamah ravu: ‘I will signal for them and gather them, for I have redeemed them; they will be as numerous as before.’ Eshrqah (I will whistle/signal) — YHWH calling his people like a shepherd whistling for his sheep or a beekeeper signaling a swarm. Isaiah 5:26 uses a different signal metaphor (YHWH raising a flag for distant nations), but the signaling of scattered peoples to return is a consistent divine-shepherd action. Pedaytim (I have redeemed them) — the redemption verb pada (used for the firstborn redeemed from death, Exodus 13:13; for Israel redeemed from Egypt, Deuteronomy 7:8) is here used proleptically: the gathering is the enactment of a redemption already accomplished in divine purpose.
9: va’ezraem ba’ammim uvemerchaqqim yizkeruni veyachyu et beneihem veshabu: ‘Though I scattered them among the nations, they will remember me even in distant countries; they will survive with their children and return.’ The divine scattering (ezraem ba’ammim) that is the instrument of punishment becomes the context of memory (yizkeruni, they will remember me). The dispersion that seemed like abandonment becomes the occasion for the diaspora’s return to YHWH — the exile’s spiritual purpose is the deepening of YHWH-remembrance at a distance. This is the OT’s explanation for why diaspora experience can strengthen rather than destroy faith: the absence from the land intensifies the longing for the covenantal God.
10: vahashivotim me’eretz Mitzraim ume’Ashur aqabbetzem ve’el eretz Gil’ad uLevanon aviam velo yimmatze lahem: ‘I will bring them back from Egypt and gather them from Assyria. I will settle them in Gilead and Lebanon until there is no room for them.’ The pairing of Egypt and Assyria as the two poles of exile represents the totality of the scattered diaspora — the Egyptian and Mesopotamian ends of the ancient Near East. Gilead (Transjordan) and Lebanon (the northern territory) are the marginal lands that will receive the overflow population of the restored community. The phrase velo yimmatze lahem (until there is no room/found for them) implies such abundance of returnees that even the border regions fill up — a reversal of the emptied land of exile.
11: ve’avar vayam tzarah vehikah vayam gallim veyavesha kol metzolelot ye’or: ‘He will pass through the sea of distress and strike down the waves; all the depths of the Nile will dry up.’ The new Exodus imagery — passing through the sea, striking down waves, drying up the Nile — applies the Exodus pattern (the Red Sea crossing) to the eschatological ingathering. The Assyrian and Egyptian powers are symbolized by their great rivers: Assyria by the Euphrates, Egypt by the Nile. YHWH drying up the Nile depths reverses the plagues (Exod 7-12) in a new register: instead of turning water to blood as judgment, he dries the rivers to create a passage for returning exiles (Isaiah 11:15-16 develops the same new-Exodus motif).
12: vegibbarti otam baYHWH uvshemo yithalaku ne’um YHWH: ‘I will strengthen them in the LORD, and they will walk in his name, declares the LORD.’ The closing verse of the chapter combines two key covenant concepts: ugebibartim baYHWH (I will strengthen them in the LORD — the covenant relationship as the source of strength, not their own power) and uvshemo yithalaku (they will walk in his name — the covenantal lifestyle of halakha, walking according to YHWH’s revealed character). The “name” (shem) of YHWH is not merely a label but the full revelation of his character and power; to walk “in the name” is to live in alignment with who YHWH is. The NT equivalent: “whatever you do in word or deed, do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus” (Colossians 3:17).
